name: NW San Juan County
short_description: BLM public land in northwestern New Mexico offering excellent rockhounding for agate, jasper, and petrified wood. No permit required - collect up to 25 lbs per day on this gently sloping high deser...
description:
NW San Juan County spans gently sloping BLM terrain at nearly 5000 feet elevation in the heart of New Mexico's rockhounding country. This remote high desert landscape is renowned for its abundance of colorful agates, banded jasper, and silicified wood scattered across washes and hillsides. The area benefits from minimal development, keeping specimens relatively accessible and abundant compared to heavily worked sites.

The geology here reflects the Basin and Range province combined with sedimentary formations from the ancestral Rocky Mountains and Laramide Orogeny. Miocene volcanic activity and subsequent silica-rich groundwater created the conditions for agate nodules and chalcedony deposits found throughout the region. Quaternary erosion has exposed these gems at the surface, particularly in arroyos and badlands formations where water action continuously uncovers fresh material.

Expect to spend your time scanning washes after rain, hillside surfaces, and outcrop areas where erosion naturally concentrates specimens. The gentle slopes make this accessible compared to rougher terrain further south, but the remoteness requires self-sufficiency. Bring plenty of water - the high desert heat and elevation require preparation. Most productive rockhounders focus on post-rain conditions when recent runoff has exposed fresh material and consolidated soil is easier to work.

This is true public land rockhounding with no fees or permits required. The 25 lb daily limit allows you to take home meaningful specimens while respecting public resource stewardship. Combine this site with nearby locations in San Juan County for a multi-day rockhounding trip through one of the Southwest's premier gem hunting regions.
history: The San Juan Basin contains Paleozoic and Mesozoic sedimentary formations uplift by Laramide tectonics, with overlying Tertiary volcanic deposits rich in silica. Silica-bearing groundwaters percolating through these formations during the Miocene epoch created the agate, chalcedony, and jasper deposits found here today, while subsequent erosion has exhumed these gems to surface visibility.
tips: Focus on arroyos and wash bottoms after recent rain when fresh material is exposed and darker soil concentrates lighter-colored agates and jasper. Look for semi-translucent pieces with banding patterns on hillside slopes - these often indicate higher-quality finds. Carry a UV light at dawn - many local agates fluoresce and are easier to spot before full sunlight. Bring a loupe to examine specimens for chalcedony color variation and internal banding that distinguishes premium finds from common material.
terrain_notes: Gentle slopes at 4931 feet elevation across open high desert with sparse desert scrub vegetation. Terrain is relatively easy walking but requires sturdy footwear due to scattered loose rock and small sharp stones. Most productive areas are 0.5 to 2 miles from parking, accessible via short hikes across exposed ground. Surface is mixed sandy soil, exposed bedrock, and scattered cobbles.
best_season: October - April. The high desert location experiences extreme summer heat (often exceeding 95 degrees F) making spring and fall ideal. Winter temperatures are moderate at this elevation. Summer monsoon activity July-September can create flash flood hazards in arroyos, and excessive heat makes midday collecting dangerous.
written_directions: From Farmington, take US 64 west approximately 25 miles toward Shiprock. Turn north onto BLM access roads in the NW San Juan County area (road numbers vary - consult BLM maps). Establish parking at any BLM-accessible pullout or parking area along established roads. High-clearance or 4x4 vehicle recommended for some secondary BLM roads; passenger vehicles can access main corridors. GPS coordinate 36.761698, -108.655334 marks a central location within this broad public land area.
road_conditions: BLM roads range from maintained dirt to rough high-clearance routes. Main access roads are passable to high-clearance or AWD vehicles. Some secondary roads may require 4x4 after rain or snow. Check current BLM road conditions before visiting.
parking_notes: Multiple informal BLM pullouts and parking areas throughout the region. No developed parking facilities. Park well off active roads to avoid blocking access. Capacity typically 2-4 vehicles per pullout.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation or mechanized removal of material. Collect only loose surface specimens and small hand-portable pieces. Do not excavate or blast. All collecting must comply with BLM public land regulations.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No mechanized removal
Collect loose surface material only
No blasting or heavy equipment
nearest_services: Farmington, NM approximately 25 miles southeast - full services including gas, food, lodging, and water. Shiprock approximately 30 miles west - limited services.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with gentle slopes and relatively flat walking areas. Uneven rocky surface with scattered small sharp stones requires sturdy hiking boots. Some areas feature loose rock and minor drop-offs; use caution. No wheelchair accessibility. Walking distances of 0.5 to 2 miles typical. Not recommended for visitors with limited mobility or significant balance concerns.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding in NW San Juan County, New Mexico?
faq.answer: The primary gems found here are agate, jasper, petrified wood, and chalcedony. Agates range from translucent banded varieties to solid chalcedony pieces in reds, yellows, and earth tones. Jasper typically displays red, brown, and tan banding. These specimens are found scattered across BLM land as surface material in washes and on hillsides. Post-rain conditions expose fresh material most readily.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound on BLM land in San Juan County?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding on BLM public land in NW San Juan County. Collection is allowed under the Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA) for personal use only. However, you must follow the 25 lb per day limit and cannot engage in commercial collection or use mechanized equipment.

faq.question: How far is the nearest town with services to NW San Juan County rockhounding areas?
faq.answer: Farmington, New Mexico is the closest full-service town, approximately 25 miles southeast of the NW San Juan County BLM rockhounding areas. Farmington offers gas, food, lodging, and water. Shiprock is approximately 30 miles west but has limited services. Plan accordingly as the rockhounding areas are remote - bring sufficient water, fuel, and supplies.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding in NW San Juan County?
faq.answer: October through April is the ideal rockhounding season. Summer temperatures regularly exceed 95 degrees F, creating dangerous heat exposure. Winter at this 4931-foot elevation remains mild. Spring and fall weather is comfortable for extended collecting. Avoid July-September due to monsoon activity creating flash flood hazards in arroyos and extreme midday heat.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding in San Juan County?
faq.answer: Bring a rock hammer, chisel, and loupe for examining specimens in the field. A UV light is useful at dawn when fluorescent agates are easier to spot. Essential safety items include sturdy hiking boots, wide-brimmed hat, and multiple water containers (at least 3-4 liters per person). High-clearance or AWD vehicle is recommended for accessing BLM roads. GPS device or offline maps are critical in this remote area.

faq.question: Is NW San Juan County rockhounding suitable for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes, this location is beginner-friendly with moderate difficulty. The gentle slopes and accessible surface material make it ideal for families with children ages 8 and up. No technical skills are required - specimens are found loose on the surface. However, the remote location and high desert heat require preparation and self-sufficiency. Start with short walks near your vehicle and bring plenty of water and sun protection.
